chemicals.concentration_notes: These toxins are concentrated in the latex (often described as a bitter yellow sap) located just under the skin of the leaves2. The concentration of these toxins is notably higher in younger leaves
chemicals.description: in cats: These compounds act as potent purgatives (laxatives). Upon ingestion, they irritate the gastrointestinal tract, leading to increased mucus production and water secretion in the colon. This results in abrupt, severe diarrhea and vomiting. Additionally, ingestion has been linked to hypoglycemia (low blood sugar) in some cases
chemicals.name: Specific glycosides identified include Barbaloin, Aloin, and Emodin. The sources also list Chrysophanic acid as a related component

chemicals.concentration_notes: Like anthraquinones, saponins are found in the leaves and latex of the plant. While the clear inner gel is often used medicinally for humans, the toxic latex layer makes chewing on the plant dangerous for cats24. Clinical Signs Summary: Signs of toxicity include vomiting, diarrhea (which may be red-tinged due to plant pigments), lethargy, anorexia, and potentially low blood sugar
chemicals.description: in cats: Saponins have detergent-like properties that cause severe irritation to the gastrointestinal lining. Ingestion typically leads to vomiting, diarrhea, and lethargy
chemicals.name: Saponins (a class of chemical compounds)

description: Aloe Vera is a common succulent houseplant valued for its medicinal gel, but the plant itself is toxic to cats. The primary toxic agents are anthraquinones (particularly aloin and barbaloin) concentrated in the yellow latex layer just beneath the outer skin, along with saponins throughout the leaf. Cats have a limited ability to metabolize these compounds, causing irritation throughout the gastrointestinal tract and, in larger exposures, systemic effects.
name: Aloe Vera
safetyNotes: Symptoms: Vomiting, diarrhea (often yellow or reddish-tinged), lethargy, loss of appetite, and low blood sugar; tremors and dark urine in more serious cases.

Gel vs. latex: The clear inner gel contains very little aloin and is far less toxic than the yellow latex layer. However, whole-leaf products or plants chewed directly expose cats to both, making ingestion of the whole plant dangerous.
Action: Keep aloe plants out of reach. Contact a vet if your cat has chewed on a leaf, especially if diarrhea or lethargy develops.

symptoms.name: Vomiting
symptoms.notes: This is caused by saponins and anthraquinone glycosides found in the plant's latex (yellow sap), which act as purgatives and irritants to the digestive tract1.... 2. Diarrhea

symptoms.name: Diarrhea
symptoms.notes: The stool may appear red-tinged; this is often due to the plant's pigments passing through the system rather than actual blood, though it can look alarming. This symptom is driven by increased mucus and water secretion in the colon caused by glycosides12. 3. Lethargy

symptoms.name: Lethargy (Depression)
symptoms.notes: This is a generalized systemic reaction to the toxins and the physical toll of gastrointestinal distress1.... 4. Hypoglycemia
symptoms.onset: Following the onset of gastrointestinal symptoms.

symptoms.name: Hypoglycemia (Low Blood Sugar)
symptoms.notes: While less common than GI signs, a drop in blood sugar has been documented in cases of Aloe barbadensis ingestion2. 5. Anorexia

symptoms.name: Anorexia (Loss of appetite)
symptoms.notes: Cats may refuse food due to the nausea and abdominal discomfort associated with the plant's purgative effects

treatments.description: This involves physically removing any remaining plant material from the cat's mouth and wiping or rinsing the oral cavity with water to remove the irritating latex/sap.
treatments.name: Decontamination (Oral Cleansing)
treatments.notes: This is the immediate first step to prevent further ingestion of the toxins (anthraquinone glycosides and saponins). Owners are advised not to induce vomiting at home unless specifically instructed by a veterinarian, as this can cause further complications.

treatments.description: The administration of fluids (intravenous or subcutaneous) to restore hydration and electrolyte balance.
treatments.name: Fluid Therapy
treatments.notes: This is critical because Aloe Vera ingestion can cause "abrupt, severe diarrhea" and vomiting, leading to rapid fluid loss and dehydration. The goal is to maintain systemic hydration while the toxins pass through the system.

treatments.description: The administration of medications to stop vomiting (antiemetics) and control diarrhea (antidiarrheals), along with gastroprotectants to coat the stomach.
treatments.name: Symptomatic Drug Therapy (Antiemetics and Antidiarrheals)
treatments.notes: Since Aloe acts as a potent purgative, controlling the severe diarrhea is a primary focus of veterinary treatment to prevent exhaustion and further fluid loss.

treatments.description: Measuring the cat's blood sugar levels.
treatments.name: Blood Glucose Monitoring
treatments.notes: Unique to Aloe barbadensis toxicity, ingestion has been linked to hypoglycemia (low blood sugar). Veterinarians may monitor this to ensure levels remain stable, treating with dextrose if necessary.

treatments.description: Feeding the cat a gentle, easily digestible diet (such as boiled chicken or prescription gastrointestinal food) once vomiting has ceased.
treatments.name: Dietary Management (Bland Diet)
treatments.notes: This helps soothe the gastrointestinal tract and allows it to recover from the irritation caused by the plant's saponins and latex.
